title: Strong systems and floor pull
transcript:
Supervisor: We’ve had Aditi for about ten weeks now. Honestly she’s been a relief.

Intern: What stands out day to day?

Supervisor: She didn’t come in with slides—she went to the line on day two. She set up a simple morning huddle in packing: five minutes, three numbers—yesterday output, today plan, one blocker. People actually show up because she kept it short.

Intern: Any concrete outcomes?

Supervisor: Dispatch reliability. We used to miss 3–4 trucks a week for paperwork or FG not staged. Last month we missed one, and that was because a supplier short-shipped us. She built a one-page checklist at the dock—who signs, what photo goes on WhatsApp—sounds small but it stuck.

Intern: How does the team respond to her?

Supervisor: The floor supervisors argue less with planning now because expectations are visible on a whiteboard. One guy, Vikram, was skeptical; he told me last Friday he’d rather have her checklist than another ERP training.

Intern: Anything she should improve?

Supervisor: She pushes hard on documentation—good for us long term—but sometimes the older supervisors feel “watched.” She’s learning to soften the framing.

Intern: Systems-wise?

Supervisor: We have a daily production log for the first time that isn’t fiction. She linked it to dispatch proof so accounts can bill faster. My CFO stopped chasing me for “pending despatch” screenshots.

Intern: Safety or compliance?

Supervisor: She ran a near-miss week—we got eight reports, which is eight more than usual. Nothing dramatic, but people started talking.

Intern: Anything we didn’t cover you want to add?

Supervisor: She’s earned trust. I want her to stay through the audit prep next quarter.

title: Reliable operator, uneven depth

metadata.clientContext: Food packaging, two shifts, seasonal demand spikes

transcript:
Supervisor: Rohan is sincere. He shows up, he stays late when we spike before Diwali.

Intern: What has improved with him onsite?

Supervisor: He fixed our overtime chaos—half the problem was we didn’t know who was actually on the line. He put a roster on the notice board and stuck to it. People stopped gaming the register.

Intern: Quality or rework?

Supervisor: Rework is still there on the pouch sealers when humidity jumps. He tried a humidity log for a week but then we got busy and it fell off.

Intern: Systems?

Supervisor: He’s good at firefighting. When a machine goes down he coordinates with maintenance fast. I don’t know if we have “systems” yet—it’s still a lot of WhatsApp groups.

Intern: How do supervisors respond to him?

Supervisor: Maintenance likes him. Production manager finds him a bit impatient—wants answers on WhatsApp at 10 p.m.

Intern: Cash or billing side?

Supervisor: We didn’t really talk about that with him. Finance does their own thing.

Intern: Safety?

Supervisor: He reminds people about gloves on the filling line. We haven’t done a formal near-miss program.

Intern: Overall?

Supervisor: I’d call him dependable for the spike season. I still need someone to make the routines survive when we’re not in panic mode.

title: Trust and follow-through concerns

metadata.clientContext: Industrial fasteners, job-shop mix, high variety

transcript:
Supervisor: I’ll be direct. We wanted structure; what we got was a lot of meetings and not enough closure.

Intern: Can you give an example?

Supervisor: She proposed a triage board for hot jobs—great idea. Two weeks later half the cards are blank and nobody updates it. When I asked, she said “the team has to own it.” I agree, but ownership doesn’t appear by itself.

Intern: Reliability?

Supervisor: She committed to a weekly summary email to me and the owner. It came maybe three times out of eight weeks. Small thing, but I stopped trusting the cadence.

Intern: Team response?

Supervisor: Mixed. Some junior engineers like her energy. Shop floor leads say she changes the format every few days so they can’t build habit.

Intern: Outcomes on delivery?

Supervisor: Dispatch is still messy. We still argue about job priorities at 6 p.m. I haven’t seen a sustained change in on-time dispatch—maybe a good day here and there.

Intern: Quality or scrap?

Supervisor: Not really her lane—we didn’t involve her in quality meetings much.

Intern: Professional conduct?

Supervisor: No issues there—she’s polite, confidential, fine on that front.

Intern: Anything positive?

Supervisor: She’s smart. When she’s in the room, problem statements get clearer. But I need fewer workshops and more locked routines.

Intern: Systems building?

Supervisor: That’s the gap. We have fragments, not a system the team runs without her pushing.
